candidate_rule.rule: When a lesion shows a central zone of structureless pinkish-to-violaceous hue surrounded by loosely arranged, fine hairpin-like or fissured whitish-grey clods (milia-like cysts or comedo-like openings in a cerebriform arrangement), set against a diffuse background of scattered light-brown granular pigmentation on pale skin, classify as Benign Keratosis.
preconditions: Central structureless zone with pinkish, purplish, or violaceous coloration (suggestive of regression or dermal melanin/vasculature typical of seborrhoeic keratosis)
Presence of whitish-grey clod-like structures arranged in a loosely cerebriform or fissured pattern at the lesion periphery or throughout (milia-like cysts and/or comedo-like openings)
Absence of atypical pigment network, irregular dots/globules, or radial streaming typical of Actinic Keratosis

Background skin showing diffuse fine granular light-brown pigmentation consistent with sun-damaged skin, but lesion itself lacks the strawberry pattern (red pseudo-network with follicular openings) of Actinic Keratosis
Overall lesion border is relatively well-demarcated with a rounded or lobulated outline rather than the ill-defined, erythematous, scaly border of Actinic Keratosis
candidate_rule.rationale: Benign Keratosis (seborrhoeic keratosis) characteristically displays milia-like cysts, comedo-like openings, and a cerebriform surface architecture in dermoscopy. The violaceous/pinkish structureless central area reflects the thick, acanthotic epidermis or invaginated keratin typical of SK. In contrast, Actinic Keratosis exhibits a strawberry pattern with erythematous pseudo-network surrounding unfilled follicular openings, surface scale, and often a rosette sign under polarized light. The combination of whitish fissured clods, lobulated outline, and absence of follicular pseudo-network reliably separates SK from AK even on sun-damaged skin.

candidate_rule.rule: When a lesion on hair-bearing skin shows a central cluster of brown-to-dark-brown pigment with a moth-eaten or lacy/reticulated internal pattern, surrounded by individual pigmented hair follicle ostia or comma-shaped brown structures, set against a pink background with visible terminal hairs, classify as Benign Keratosis (seborrhoeic keratosis or lentigo-type benign keratosis).
preconditions: Lesion is located on hair-bearing skin with multiple visible terminal hairs traversing the field
Central pigmented area forms a discrete, roughly oval cluster rather than diffuse ill-defined spread across the entire field

Pigment within the cluster shows variable brown tones (light brown to dark brown/black) with an irregular but internally structured, lacy or reticulated arrangement — not a homogeneous structureless blush
Small dark brown puncta or comma-shaped structures consistent with pigmented follicular openings are visible at the periphery or within the cluster
Background skin shows a pink, slightly vascular hue but WITHOUT the diffuse strawberry-pattern vessels, white circles around follicles, or surface scale typical of actinic keratosis
No clearly defined surface scale, erythematous follicular halos, or rosette structures that would indicate actinic keratosis
candidate_rule.rationale: Actinic keratosis on hair-bearing skin typically displays a 'strawberry pattern' — diffuse erythema, white perifollicular halos, and surface scale — with structureless pink-red pigmentation. In contrast, this image shows a focal, discrete cluster of variably pigmented brown structures with an internal lacy texture and pigmented follicular units, which is characteristic of a benign keratosis (seborrhoeic keratosis or solar lentigo variant) on hair-bearing skin. The discreteness and internal structure of the pigment cluster, combined with the absence of perifollicular white halos or scale, are the key features that should override a misclassification driven purely by the pink, sun-damaged background.
